original_abstract: Sodium-ion batteries have emerged as a frontrunner in electrochemical energy storage, yet achieving high energy density remains difficult due to the significant structural strain induced during (de)sodiation. To address this, we developed a P2-layered cathode, Na<sub>0.7</sub>Li<sub>0.03</sub>[Mg<sub>0.15</sub>Li<sub>0.07</sub>Mn<sub>0.75</sub>]O<sub>2</sub> (NMLMO), featuring a unique dual-site lithium substitution where Li ions occupy both transition-metal (TM) and alkali-metal (AM) sites. Integrating theoretical modeling with experimental insights, we demonstrate that TM-site lithium facilitates Na-O-Li electronic configurations that trigger robust oxygen anionic redox, significantly boosting capacity. Simultaneously, AM-site lithium acts as a structural "pillar" within the LiO<sub>6</sub> prisms, suppressing detrimental phase transitions and stabilizing the lattice. Consequently, the NMLMO cathode achieves a high specific capacity of 266 mAh g<sup>-1</sup> while maintaining a nearly zero-strain characteristic across a broad voltage window of 1.5–4.6 V. These results underscore dual-site substitution as a powerful strategy for overcoming the inherent trade-off between capacity and stability in next-generation battery materials.
humanized_abstract: Sodium-ion batteries (SIBs) are considered to be one of the potential electrochemical energy storage systems, but the high energy density is still hard to achieve due to the high structural strain caused by the (de)sodiation process. In this study, we designed a novel P2-layered cathode material Na0.7Li0.03[Mg0.15Li0.07Mn0.75]O2 (NMLMO), in which dual-site lithium substitution is realized, and Li ions are uniformly distributed in both transition-metal (TM) sites and alkali-metal (AM) sites. Based on the integrated theoretical simulations and experimental results, it is confirmed that TM-site lithium realizes the Na-O-Li synergistic state in the TMO2 layer, which effectively triggers the robust oxygen anionic redox, thus realizes high capacity. Meanwhile, AM-site lithium acts as the structural “pillar” within the LiO6 prisms, preventing phase transformation and maintaining the stability of the cathode structure. As a result, a specific discharge capacity as high as 266 mAh g−1 can be realized while maintaining the cathode material with near zero-strain characteristic within the wide operating voltage window of 1.5–4.6 V. Therefore, the strategy of dual-site lithium substitution realizes the sacrifice of energy and power density to material stability, which provides a possible solution to balance the performance of next-generation SIBs.

Based on the integrated theoretical simulations and experimental results, it is confirmed that TM-site lithium realizes the Na-O-Li synergistic state in the TMO2 layer, which effectively triggers the robust oxygen anionic redox, thus realizes high capacity.
